## RANDOM FEATURE ATTENTION

**Hao Peng** _[♠∗]_ **Nikolaos Pappas** _[♠]_ **Dani Yogatama** _[♣]_ **Roy Schwartz** _[♥]_ **Noah A. Smith** _[♠♦]_ **Lingpeng Kong**[♦] _[∗]_

> _♠_ Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, University of Washington

> _♣_ DeepMind _♦_ Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence

> _♥_ School of Computer Science & Engineering, Hebrew University of Jerusalem

♦Department of Computer Science , The University of Hong Kong
_{_ hapeng,npappas,nasmith _}_ @cs.washington.edu dyogatama@google.com, roys@cs.huji.ac.il, lpk@cs.hku.hk

## ABSTRACT

Transformers are state-of-the-art models for a variety of sequence modeling tasks. At their core is an attention function which models pairwise interactions between the inputs at every timestep. While attention is powerful, it does _not_ scale efficiently to long sequences due to its quadratic time and space complexity in the sequence length. We propose RFA, a linear time and space **a** ttention that uses **r** andom **f** eature methods to approximate the softmax function, and explore its application in transformers. RFA can be used as a drop-in replacement for conventional softmax attention and offers a straightforward way of learning with recency bias through an optional gating mechanism. Experiments on language modeling and machine translation demonstrate that RFA achieves similar or better performance compared to strong transformer baselines. In the machine translation experiment, RFA decodes twice as fast as a vanilla transformer. Compared to existing efficient transformer variants, RFA is competitive in terms of both accuracy and efficiency on three long text classification datasets. Our analysis shows that RFA’s efficiency gains are especially notable on long sequences, suggesting that RFA will be particularly useful in tasks that require working with large inputs, fast decoding speed, or low memory footprints.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017) have achieved tremendous success on a variety of sequence modeling tasks (Ott et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018; Parmar et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Parisotto et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ). Under the hood, the key component is attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015), which models pairwise interactions of the inputs, regardless of their distances from each other. This comes with quadratic time and memory costs, making the transformers computationally expensive, especially for long sequences. A large body of research has been devoted to improving their time and memory efficiency (Tay et al., 2020c). Although better _asymptotic_ complexity and prominent gains for long sequences have been achieved (Lee et al., 2019; Child et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ), in practice, many existing approaches are less well-suited for moderatelength ones: the additional computation steps required by some approaches can overshadow the time and memory they save (Kitaev et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ).

This work proposes **r** andom **f** eature **a** ttention (RFA), an efficient attention variant that scales linearly in sequence length in terms of time and space, and achieves practical gains for both long and moderate length sequences. RFA builds on a kernel perspective of softmax (Rawat et al., 2019). Using the well-established random feature maps (Rahimi & Recht, 2007; Avron et al., 2016; _§_ 2), RFA approximates the dot-then-exponentiate function with a kernel trick (Hofmann et al., 2008): exp( **x** _·_ **y** ) _≈_ _**φ**_ ( **x** ) _·_ _**φ**_ ( **y** ). Inspired by its connections to gated recurrent neural networks (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014) and fast weights (Schmidhuber, 1992), we further augment RFA with an optional gating mechanism, offering a straightforward way of learning with recency bias when locality is desired.

> _∗_ The majority of this work was done while these authors were at DeepMind.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

RFA and its gated variant ( _§_ 3) can be used as a drop-in substitute for the canonical softmax attention, and increase the number of parameters by less than 0.1%. We explore its applications in transformers on language modeling, machine translation, and long text classification ( _§_ 4). Our experiments show that RFA achieves comparable performance to vanilla transformer baselines in all tasks, while outperforming a recent related approach (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). The gating mechanism proves particularly useful in language modeling: the gated variant of RFA outperforms the transformer baseline on WikiText-103. RFA shines in decoding, even for shorter sequences. In our head-to-head comparison on machine translation benchmarks, RFA decodes around 2 _×_ faster than a transformer baseline, _without_ accuracy loss. Comparisons to several recent efficient transformer variants on three long text classification datasets show that RFA is competitive in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Our analysis ( _§_ 5) shows that more significant time and memory efficiency improvements can be achieved for longer sequences: 12 _×_ decoding speedup with less than 10% of the memory for 2,048-length outputs.

## 2 BACKGROUND

## 2.1 ATTENTION IN SEQUENCE MODELING

The attention mechanism (Bahdanau et al., 2015) has been widely used in many sequence modeling tasks. Its dot-product variant is the key building block for the state-of-the-art transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017). Let _{_ **q** _t}[N] t_ =1[denote a sequence of] _[ N]_ **[query]**[ vectors,][that attend to] sequences of _M_ **key** and **value** vectors. At each timestep, the attention linearly combines the values weighted by the outputs of a softmax:
_τ_ is the temperature hyperparameter determining how “flat” the softmax is (Hinton et al., 2015).[1]

Calculating attention for a single query takes _O_ ( _M_ ) time and space. For the full sequence of _N_ queries the space amounts to _O_ ( _MN_ ). When the computation _cannot_ be parallelized across the queries, e.g., in autoregressive decoding, the time complexity is quadratic in the sequence length.

## 2.2 RANDOM FEATURE METHODS

The theoretical backbone of this work is the unbiased estimation of the Gaussian kernel by Rahimi & Recht (2007). Based on Bochner’s theorem (Bochner, 1955), Rahimi & Recht (2007) proposed random Fourier features to approximate a desired shift-invariant kernel. The method nonlinearly transforms a pair of vectors **x** and **y** using a **random feature map** _**φ**_ ; the inner product between _**φ**_ ( **x** ) and _**φ**_ ( **y** ) approximates the kernel evaluation on **x** and **y** . More precisely:
**Theorem 1** (Rahimi & Recht, 2007) **.** _Let_ _**φ**_ : R _[d] →_ R[2] _[D] be a nonlinear transformation:_

Variance of the estimation is inversely proportional to _D_ (Appendix A.2; Yu et al., 2016).

Random feature methods proved successful in speeding up kernel methods (Oliva et al., 2015; Avron et al., 2017; Sun, 2019, _inter alia_ ), and more recently are used to efficiently approximate softmax (Rawat et al., 2019). In _§_ 3.1, we use it to derive an unbiased estimate to exp( _⟨_ _**·** ,_ _**·** ⟩_ ) and then an efficient approximation to softmax attention.

## 3 MODEL

This section presents RFA ( _§_ 3.1) and its gated variant ( _§_ 3.2). In _§_ 3.3 we lay out several design choices and relate RFA to prior works. We close by practically analyzing RFA’s complexity ( _§_ 3.4).

3.1 RANDOM FEATURE ATTENTION

Analogously to the softmax attention, RFA has its multiheaded variant (Vaswani et al., 2017). In our experiments we use causal RFA in a transformer language model ( _§_ 4.1), and both cross and causal RFA in the decoder of a sequence-to-sequence machine translation model.

- 3.2 RFA-GATE: LEARNING WITH RECENCY BIAS

The canonical softmax attention does _not_ have any explicit modeling of distance or locality. In learning problems where such inductive bias is crucial (Ba et al., 2016; Parmar et al., 2018; Miconi et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019, _inter alia_ ), transformers heavily rely on positional encodings. Answering to this, many approaches have been proposed, e.g., learning the attention spans (Sukhbaatar et al.,

2019; Wu et al., 2020), and enhancing the attention computation with recurrent (Hao et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019) or convolutional (Wu et al., 2019; Mohamed et al., 2019) components.

RFA faces the same issue, but its causal attention variant (Eq. 6) offers a straightforward way of learning with recency bias. We draw inspiration from its connections to RNNs, and augment RFA with a learned gating mechanism (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014; Peng et al., 2018, _inter alia_ ):

**w** _g_ and _bg_ are learned parameters, and **x** _t_ is the input representation at timestep _t_ .[4] By multiplying the learned scalar gates 0 _< gt <_ 1 against the hidden state ( **S** _t,_ **z** _t_ ), history is exponentially decayed, favoring more recent context.

The gating mechanism shows another benefit of RFA: it would be otherwise more difficult to build similar techniques into the softmax attention, where there is no clear sense of “recurrence” (Appendix A.5). It proves useful in our language modeling experiments ( _§_ 4.1).

## 3.3 DISCUSSION

**On query and key norms, and learned random feature variance.** Eq. 5 assumes both the query and keys are of norm-1. It therefore approximates a softmax attention that normalizes the queries and keys before multiplying them, and then scales the logits by dividing them by _σ_[2] . Empirically, this normalization step scales down the logits (Vaswani et al., 2017) and enforces that _−_ 1 _≤_ **q** _[⊤]_ **k** _≤_ 1. In consequence, the softmax outputs would be “flattened” if not for _σ_ , which can be set _a priori_ as a hyperparameter (Yu et al., 2016; Avron et al., 2017; Sun, 2019, _inter alia_ ). Here we instead learn it from data with the reparameterization trick (Kingma & Welling, 2014):
**I** vector _d_ is the _**σ** d_ is learned, but random vectors _× d_ identity matrix, and _◦_ denotes elementwise product between vectors. **w** � _i_ are _not_ .[5] _d_ -dimensional

This norm-1 constraint is never mandatory. Rather, we employ it for notation clarity and easier implementation. In preliminary experiments we find it has little impact on the performance when _σ_ is set properly or learned from data. Eq. 12 in Appendix A presents RFA _without_ imposing it.

**Going beyond the Gaussian kernel.** More broadly, random feature methods can be applied to a family of shift-invariant kernels, with the Gaussian kernel being one of them. In the same family, the order-1 arc-cosine kernel (Cho & Saul, 2009) can be approximated with feature map: _**φ**_ arccos( **x** ) = ~~�~~ 1 _/D_ [ReLU( **w** 1 _·_ **x** ) _, . . . ,_ ReLU( **w** _D ·_ **x** )] _[⊤]_ (Alber et al., 2017).[6] In our experiments, the Gaussian and arc-cosine variants achieve similar performance. This supplements the exploration of alternatives to softmax in attention (Tsai et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2019).

**Relations to prior work.** Katharopoulos et al. (2020) inspire the causal attention variant of RFA. They use a feature map based on the exponential linear unit activation (Clevert et al., 2016): elu( _**·**_ ) + 1. It significantly _underperforms_ both the baseline and RFA in our controlled experiments, showing the importance of a properly-chosen feature map. Random feature approximation of attention is also explored by a concurrent work (Choromanski et al., 2021), with applications in masked language modeling for proteins. They propose positive random features to approximate softmax, aiming for a lower variance in critical regions. RFA instead normalizes the queries and keys before random projection to reduce variance. Going beyond both, RFA establishes the benefits of random feature methods as a more universal substitute for softmax across all attention variants, facilitating its applications in, e.g., sequence-to-sequence learning.

There are interesting connections between gated RFA and fast weights (Schmidhuber, 1992; 1993; Ba et al., 2016; Miconi et al., 2018, _inter alia_ ). Emphasizing recent patterns, they learn a temporal memory to store history similarly to Eqs. 7. The main difference is that RFA additionally normalizes the output using _**φ**_ ( **q** _t_ ) _·_ **z** as in Eq. 6, a by-product of approximating softmax’s partition function. It is intriguing to study the role of this normalization term, which we leave to future work.

## 3.4 COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS

**Time.** Scaling linearly in the sequence lengths, RFA needs less computation (in terms of number of operations) for long sequences. This implies speedup wherever the quadratic-time softmax attention _cannot_ be fully-parallelized across time steps. More specifically:

- Significant speedup can be expected in autoregressive _decoding_ , both conditional (e.g., machine translation) and unconditional (e.g., sampling from a language model). For example, 1.9 _×_ speedup is achieved in our machine translation experiments ( _§_ 4.2); and more for longer sequences (e.g., 12 _×_ for 2,048-length ones; _§_ 5).

- Some applications (e.g., language modeling, text classification) reveal inputs to the model in full.[7] When there are enough threads to parallelize softmax attention across time steps, hardly any speedup from RFA can be achieved; when there are not, typically for very long sequences ( _>_ 1,000), substantial speed gain is possible. For example, RFA does _not_ achieve any speedup when working with 512-length context ( _§_ 4.1), but achieves a 5 _._ 3 _×_ speedup with 4,000-length context ( _§_ 4.2).

**Memory.** Asymptotically, RFA has a better memory efficiency than its softmax counterpart (linear vs. quadratic). To reach a more practical conclusion, we include in our analysis the cost of the feature maps. _**φ**_ ’s memory overhead largely depends on its size _D_ . For example, let’s consider the cross attention of a decoder. RFA uses _O_ (4 _D_ +2 _Dd_ ) space to store _**φ**_ ( **q** _t_ ),[�] _i_ _**[φ]**_[(] **[k]** _[i]_[)] _[⊗]_ **[v]** _[i]_[, and][ �] _i_ _**[φ]**_[(] **[k]** _[i]_[)] (Eq. 5; line 12 of Algo. 2).[8] In contrast, softmax cross attention stores the encoder outputs with _O_ ( _Md_ ) memory, with _M_ being the source length. In this case RFA has a lower memory overhead when 2 _D ≪ M_ . Typically _D_ should be no less than _d_ in order for reasonable approximation (Yu et al., 2016); In a transformer model, _d_ is the size of an attention head, which is usually around 64 or 128 (Vaswani et al., 2017; Ott et al., 2018). This suggests that RFA can achieve significant memory saving with longer sequences, which is supported by our empirical analysis in _§_ 5. Further, using moderate sized feature maps is also desirable, so that its overhead does not overshadow the time and memory RFA saves. We experiment with _D_ at _d_ and 2 _d_ ; the benefit of using _D >_ 2 _d_ is marginal.
Appendix A.6 discusses the time and space complexity in more detail, and Appendix C.2 studies the effect of random feature size on performance.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate RFA on language modeling, machine translation, and long text classification.

## 4.1 LANGUAGE MODELING

**Setting.** We experiment with WikiText-103 (Merity et al., 2017). It is based on English Wikipedia. Table 5 in Appendix B summarizes some of its statistics. We compare the following models:
- BASE is our implementation of the strong transformer-based language model by Baevski & Auli (2019).
- RFA builds on BASE, but replaces the softmax attention with random feature attention. We experiment with both Gaussian and arc-cosine kernel variants.
- RFA-GATE additionally learns a sigmoid gate on top of RFA ( _§_ 3.2). It also has a Gaussian kernel variant and a arc-cosine kernel one.[9]
- _**φ**_ elu is a baseline to RFA. Instead of the random feature methods it uses the elu( _**·**_ ) + 1 feature map, as in Katharopoulos et al. (2020).

- 7A causal masking is usually used to prevent the model from accessing future tokens in language models. 8 RFA _never_ constructs the _M ×_ 2 _D × d_ tensor [ _**φ**_ ( **k** _i_ ) _⊗_ **v** _i_ ] _i_ , but sequentially processes the sequence.
> 9 This gating technique is specific to RFA variants, in the sense that it is less intuitive to apply it in BASE.

To ensure fair comparisons, we use comparable implementations, tuning, and training procedure. All models use a 512 block size during both training and evaluation, i.e., they read as input a segment of 512 consecutive tokens, _without_ access to the context from previous mini-batches. RFA variants use 64-dimensional random feature maps. We experiment with two model size settings, **small** (around 38M parameters) and **big** (around 242M parameters); they are described in Appendix B.1 along with other implementation details.
Table 1: Language model perplexity (lower is better) on the WikiText-103 development and test sets. Bolded numbers outperform BASE.

**Results.** Table 1 compares the models’ performance in perplexity on WikiText-103 development and test data. Both kernel variants of RFA, _without_ gating, outperform _**φ**_ elu by more than 2.4 and 2.1 test perplexity for the small and big model respectively, confirming the benefits from using random feature approximation.[10] Yet both _underperform_ BASE, with RFA-Gaussian having a smaller gap. Comparing RFA against its gated variants, a more than 1.8 perplexity improvement can be attributed to the gating mechanism; and the gap is larger for small models. Notably, RFA-GATE-Gaussian outperforms BASE under both size settings by at least 1.2 perplexity. In general, RFA models with Gaussian feature maps outperform their arc-cosine counterparts.[11] From the analysis in _§_ 3.4 we would _not_ expect speedup by RFA models, nor do we see any in the experiments.[12]

Closing this section, we explore a “stateful” variant of RFA-GATE-Gaussian. It passes the last hidden state ( **S** _t,_ **z** _t_ ) to the next mini-batch during both training and evaluation, a technique commonly used in RNN language models (Merity et al., 2018). This is a consequence of RFA’s RNN-style computation, and is less straightforward to be applicable in the vanilla transformer models.[13] From the last row of Table 1 we see that this brings a more than 1.5 test perplexity improvement.

## 4.2 MACHINE TRANSLATION

**Datasets.** We experiment with three standard machine translation datasets.

- WMT14 EN-DE and EN-FR (Bojar et al., 2014). Our data split and preprocessing follow those of Vaswani et al. (2017). We share the source and target vocabularies within each language pair, with 32,768 byte pair encoding types (BPE; Sennrich et al., 2016).
- IWSLT14 DE-EN (Cettolo et al., 2014) is based on TED talks. The preprocessing follows Edunov et al. (2018). Separate vocabularies of 9K/7K BPE types are used for the source and target.

Table 5 in Appendix B summarizes some statistics of the datasets.

**Setting.** We compare the RFA variants described in _§_ 4.1. They build on a BASE model that is our implementation of the base-sized transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). All RFA models apply random feature attention in decoder cross and causal attention, but use softmax attention in encoders. This setting yields the greatest decoding time and memory savings ( _§_ 3.4). We use 128/64 for _D_ in cross/causal attention. RFA-GATE learns sigmoid gates in the decoder causal attention. The _**φ**_ elu baseline uses the same setting and applies feature map in both decoder cross and causal attention, but _not_ in the encoders. Further details are described in Appendix B.2.
Table 2: Machine translation test set BLEU. The decoding speed (last column) is relative to BASE. All models are tested on a single TPU v2 accelerator, with batch size 32.

**Results.** Table 2 compares the models’ test set BLEU on three machine translation datasets. Overall both Gaussian and arc-cosine variants of RFA achieve similar performance to BASE on all three datasets, significantly outperforming Katharopoulos et al. (2020). Differently from the trends in the language modeling experiments, here the gating mechanism does not lead to substantial gains. Notably, all RFA variants decode more than 1 _._ 8 _×_ faster than BASE.

## 4.3 LONG TEXT CLASSIFICATION

We further evaluate RFA’s accuracy and efficiency when used as text encoders on three NLP tasks from the recently proposed Long Range Arena benchmark (Tay et al., 2021), designed to evaluate efficient Transformer variants on tasks that require processing long sequences.[14]

**Experimental setting and datasets.** We compare RFA against baselines on the following datasets:

- ListOps ( **LO** ; Nangia & Bowman, 2018) aims to diagnose the capability of modelling hierarchically structured data. Given a sequence of operations on single-digit integers, the model predicts the solution, also a single-digit integer. It is formulated as a 10-way classification. We follow Tay et al. (2021) and consider sequences with 500–2,000 symbols.
- Character-level text classification with the **IMDb** movie review dataset (Maas et al., 2011). This is a binary sentiment classification task.
- Character-level document retrieval with the ACL Anthology Network ( **AAN** ; Radev et al., 2009) dataset. The model classifies whether there is a citation between a pair of papers.

To ensure fair comparisons, we implement RFA on top of the transformer baseline by Tay et al. (2021), and closely follow their preprocessing, data split, model size, and training procedure. Speed and memory are evaluated on the IMDb dataset. For our RFA model, we use _D_ = 64 for the IMDb dataset, and _D_ = 128 for others. We refer the readers to Tay et al. (2021) for further details.

**Results.** From Table 3 we can see that RFA outperforms the transformer baseline on two out of the three datasets, achieving the best performance on IMDb with 66% accuracy. Averaging across three datasets, RFA outperforms the transformer by 0.3% accuracy, second only to Zaheer et al. (2020) with a 0.1% accuracy gap. In terms of time and memory efficiency, RFA is among the strongest. RFA speeds up over the transformer by 1 _._ 1–5 _._ 3 _×_ , varying by sequence length. Importantly, compared to the only two baselines that perform comparably to the baseline transformer model (Tay et al., 2020a; Zaheer et al., 2020), RFA has a clear advantage in both speed and memory efficiency, and is the only model that is competitive in both accuracy and efficiency.

## 5 ANALYSIS

**Decoding time and memory varying by sequence length.** _§_ 3.4 shows that RFA can potentially achieve more significant speedup and memory saving for longer sequences, which we now explore.

We use a simulation conditional generation experiment on to compare RFA’s sequence-to-sequence decoding speed and memory overhead against the baseline’s. Here we assume the input and output sequences are of the same length. The compared models are of the same size as those described in _§_ 4.2, with 6-layer encoders and decoders. Other hyperparameters are summarized in Appendix B.2. All models are tested using greedy decoding with the same batch size of 16, on a TPU v2 accelerator.

From Figures 2 (a) and (b) we observe clear trends. Varying the lengths, both RFA variants achieve consistent decoding speed with nearly-constant memory overhead. In contrast, the baseline decodes slower for longer sequences, taking an increasing amount of memory. Notably, for 2,048-length sequences, RFA decodes around 12 _×_ faster than the baseline while using less than 10% of the memory. RFA-arccos slightly outperforms RFA-Gaussian in terms of speed and memory efficiency. This is because when using the same _D_ (as we do here), the _**φ**_ arccos is half the size of _**φ**_ Gaussian. These results suggest that RFA can be particularly useful in sequence-to-sequence tasks with longer sequences, e.g., document-level machine translation (Miculicich et al., 2018).
Figure 3 in Appendix C.1 compares the speed and memory consumption in _unconditional_ decoding (e.g., sampling from a language model). The overall trends are similar to those in Figure 2.
**Notes on decoding speed.** With a lower memory overhead, RFA can use a larger batch size than the baseline. As noted by Katharopoulos et al. (2020) and Kasai et al. (2021), if we had used mini-

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

batches as large as the hardware allows, RFA could have achieved a more significant speed gain. Nonetheless, we control for batch size even though it is not the most favorable setting for RFA, since the conclusion translates better to common applications where one generates a single sequence at a time (e.g., instantaneous machine translation). For the softmax attention baseline, we follow Ott et al. (2018) and cache previously computed query/key/value representations, which significantly improves its decoding speed (over not caching).

**Further analysis results.** RFA achieves comparable performance to softmax attention. Appendix C.3 empirically shows that this _cannot_ be attributed to RFA learning a good approximation to softmax: when we train with one attention but evaluate with the other, the performance is hardly better than randomly-initialized untrained models. Yet, an RFA model initialized from a pretrained softmax transformer achieves decent training loss after a moderate amount of finetuning steps (Appendix C.4). This suggests some potential applications, e.g., transferring knowledge from a pretrained transformer (e.g., GPT-3; Brown et al., 2020) to an RFA model that is more efficient to sample from.

## 6 RELATED WORK

One common motivation across the following studies, that is shared by this work and the research we have already discussed, is to scale transformers to long sequences. Note that there are plenty orthogonal choices for improving efficiency such as weight sharing (Dehghani et al., 2019), quantization (Shen et al., 2020), knowledge distillation (Sanh et al., 2020), and adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019). For a detailed overview we refer the reader to Tay et al. (2020c).

**Sparse attention patterns.** The idea behind these methods is to limit the reception field of attention computation. It motivates earlier attempts in improving attention’s efficiency, and still receives lots of interest. The sparse patterns can be set _a priori_ (Liu et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2020; Ho et al., 2020; You et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ) or learned from data (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ). For most of these approaches, it is yet to be empirically verified that they are suitable for large-scale sequence-to-sequence learning; few of them have recorded decoding speed benefits.

**Compressed context.** Wang et al. (2020) compress the context along the timesteps so that the effective sequence length for attention computation is reduced. Another line of work aims to store past context into a memory module with limited size (Lee et al., 2019; Ainslie et al., 2020; Rae et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ), so that accessing longer history only moderately increases the overhead. Reminiscent of RNN language models, RFA attends beyond a fixed context window through a stateful computation, _without_ increasing time or memory overhead.

## 7 CONCLUSION

We presented random feature attention (RFA). It views the softmax attention through the lens of kernel methods, and approximates it with random feature methods. With an optional gating mechanism, RFA provides a straightforward way of learning with recency bias. RFA’s time and space complexity is linear in the sequence length. We use RFA as a drop-in substitute for softmax attention in transformer models. On language modeling, machine translation, and long text classification benchmarks, RFA achieves comparable or better performance than strong baselines. In the machine translation experiment, RFA decodes twice as fast. Further time and memory efficiency improvements can be achieved for longer sequences.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is written with the clarity, rigor, and structural precision characteristic of high-quality machine learning research. The authors effectively motivate the research gap—namely, that while many "efficient" transformer variants exist, they often fail to deliver practical gains for moderate-length sequences due to overhead—and they clearly position Random Feature Attention (RFA) as a robust, linear-time, and drop-in solution. The theoretical derivation in the provided sections is well-grounded in established kernel methods and Bochner’s theorem, and the organization is logical, moving seamlessly from the limitations of canonical attention to the mathematical underpinnings of the proposed approximation. A potential point of concern for a reviewer would be the sensitivity of the performance to the random feature maps and the hyperparameters associated with the gating mechanism; however, the authors appear to have anticipated this by including a dedicated analysis section. Overall, the work exhibits a high degree of technical competence and scientific maturity suitable for a top-tier venue like ICLR.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a timely and well-motivated contribution to efficient sequence modeling by introducing Random Feature Attention (RFA), which successfully reframes softmax attention through the lens of kernel random feature approximations to achieve linear time and space complexity. The writing is generally clear and logically organized, effectively bridging theoretical kernel methods with practical transformer design, and the empirical claims of competitive accuracy alongside substantial decoding speedups and memory reductions are compelling. However, several significant issues require clarification and stronger validation prior to publication: (1) the assertion that RFA increases parameters by less than 0.1% while employing random feature projections warrants a more rigorous explanation of the parameterization strategy, as fixed random projections typically sacrifice representational capacity, and any learned components must be carefully accounted for in the efficiency claims; (2) the inherent variance of the random feature approximation (inversely proportional to the number of features D) can introduce gradient noise that may destabilize training in deep or stacked transformer architectures, yet the paper provides limited discussion on convergence stability, variance reduction techniques, or sensitivity to D; and (3) while the optional gating mechanism is introduced as a practical remedy for locality and recency bias, its empirical necessity, hyperparameter sensitivity, and interaction with the random feature approximation remain underexplored, necessitating more systematic ablations. Overall, the work is conceptually sound and promising, but would benefit from deeper theoretical grounding on training dynamics, tighter documentation of the parameter efficiency claims, and more thorough empirical validation to fully substantiate its accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in diverse scaling regimes.

comments.title: Numerical contradiction between text claim and Table 1
comments.quote: Notably, RFA-GATE-Gaussian outperforms BASE under both size settings by at least 1.2 perplexity.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the gated Gaussian variant outperforms the BASE model by at least 1.2 perplexity for both small and big models. However, Table 1 reports a test perplexity of 26.3 for RFA-GATE-Gaussian (Big) versus 26.2 for BASE (Big), meaning it actually underperforms by 0.1 on the big test set. The claim correctly holds for the small models, but the explicit phrasing 'under both size settings' creates a direct factual contradiction with the provided data. This should be corrected to accurately reflect the big model results.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a timely and well-motivated contribution to efficient sequence modeling by introducing Random Feature Attention (RFA), which successfully reframes softmax attention through the lens of kernel random feature approximations to achieve linear time and space complexity. The writing is generally clear and logically organized, effectively bridging theoretical kernel methods with practical transformer design, and the empirical claims of competitive accuracy alongside substantial decoding speedups and memory reductions are compelling. However, several significant issues require clarification and stronger validation prior to publication: (1) the assertion that RFA increases parameters by less than 0.1% while employing random feature projections warrants a more rigorous explanation of the parameterization strategy, as fixed random projections typically sacrifice representational capacity, and any learned components must be carefully accounted for in the efficiency claims; (2) the inherent variance of the random feature approximation (inversely proportional to the number of features D) can introduce gradient noise that may destabilize training in deep or stacked transformer architectures, yet the paper provides limited discussion on convergence stability, variance reduction techniques, or sensitivity to D; and (3) while the optional gating mechanism is introduced as a practical remedy for locality and recency bias, its empirical necessity, hyperparameter sensitivity, and interaction with the random feature approximation remain underexplored, necessitating more systematic ablations. Overall, the work is conceptually sound and promising, but would benefit from deeper theoretical grounding on training dynamics, tighter documentation of the parameter efficiency claims, and more thorough empirical validation to fully substantiate its accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in diverse scaling regimes.

Variance of the estimation is inversely proportional to _D_ (Appendix A.2; Yu et al., 2016).

RFA builds on an unbiased estimate to exp( _⟨_ _**·** ,_ _**·** ⟩_ ) from Theorem 1, which we begin with:

The last line does _not_ have any nonlinear interaction between _**φ**_ ( **x** ) and _**φ**_ ( **y** ), allowing for a linear time/space approximation to attention.
comments.explanation: The explicit mathematical definition of the random feature map φ(x) is completely missing from the OCR extraction, appearing only as a garbled header followed by a variance claim. Similarly, the RFA approximation equation in §3.1 and the cross-attention softmax formula in §2.1 are absent. Without these definitions, readers cannot verify the exact feature map construction, the temperature scaling factor (σ² vs τ), or the derivation of the linear kernel trick. While these are likely standard RFF formulations lost to PDF-to-text extraction, their absence creates an underspecified method gap that hinders reproducibility and makes it difficult to verify the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Inconsistent table formatting and column alignment in Table 1
comments.explanation: The table header defines three logical columns: Model, Small (Dev/Test), and Big (Dev/Test). However, the final row for the 'Stateful' variant contains four separate numerical values, causing a misalignment with the preceding rows and the header. It appears the Dev/Test values were split into separate columns instead of being grouped under 'Small' and 'Big' as in the other rows. This formatting issue could mislead readers into thinking there are four distinct metrics for this variant, or cause confusion when parsing the results. The numbers likely correspond to Small-Dev, Small-Test, Big-Dev, and Big-Test, but the layout obscures this mapping.

comments.title: Tension between stated motivation and implementation for query/key normalization
comments.quote:
RFA instead normalizes the queries and keys before random projection to reduce variance.

...

This norm-1 constraint is never mandatory. Rather, we employ it for notation clarity and easier implementation.
comments.explanation: The authors initially present normalization as a variance-reduction technique (which is theoretically sound, as bounding input norms controls the RFF kernel variance), but immediately follow up by stating the constraint is 'never mandatory' and is used primarily for 'notation clarity'. This creates a slight contradiction between the claimed methodological benefit and the actual implementation rationale. A careful reader might question whether normalization is practically necessary for performance or merely a theoretical convenience. Clarifying whether this step is retained in final experiments specifically for numerical stability or variance control would strengthen the justification.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of the gating mechanism application
comments.quote: By multiplying the learned scalar gates 0 _< gt <_ 1 against the hidden state ( **S** _t,_ **z** _t_ ), history is exponentially decayed, favoring more recent context.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'scalar gates' but does not specify how they are applied across multi-head attention dimensions or whether they are token-level, head-level, or sequence-level scalars. In multi-head RFA, the hidden state `(S_t, z_t)` typically has shape `(heads, 2D)` or `(heads, 2D, d)`. Describing `g_t` simply as a 'scalar' without clarifying the broadcasting or per-head application could leave readers uncertain about the exact architectural placement and dimensionality of the gating operation. While likely standard practice, specifying whether `g_t` is computed per-head or elementwise would improve reproducibility.

comments.title: OCR-induced omission of key derivation equation in Section 3.1
comments.quote:
RFA builds on an unbiased estimate to exp( _⟨_ _**·** ,_ _**·** ⟩_ ) from Theorem 1, which we begin with:

The last line does _not_ have any nonlinear interaction between _**φ**_ ( **x** ) and _**φ**_ ( **y** ), allowing for a linear time/space approximation to attention.

The central equation (likely approximating the softmax partition function and numerator using the random feature map) is missing due to PDF extraction/OCR limitations. While this breaks the immediate logical flow, the subsequent text and standard RFA literature make the intended step obvious. It is flagged as an extraction artifact rather than an author error, but it creates a reading difficulty that a careful reviewer should be aware of.

comments.title: Typographical error in gate parameter notation
comments.quote: **w** _g_ and _bg_ are learned parameters, and **x** _t_ is the input representation at timestep _t_ .
comments.explanation: The bias term is written as '_bg_' without a subscript separator, clearly intended to be '_b_g_'. This is a minor typesetting/OCR artifact that does not affect the mathematical meaning but could momentarily confuse a reader parsing the notation or attempting to reproduce the gating mechanism.

comments.title: Ambiguity in space complexity description for cross-attention
comments.explanation: The text states RFA stores the sums `Σ_i φ(k_i) ⊗ v_i` and `Σ_i φ(k_i)`. While mathematically these sums collapse into fixed-size tensors (`2D × d` and `2D` respectively), the phrasing 'store Σ_i...' could misleadingly suggest storing `M` individual terms or a growing tensor. Footnote 8 correctly clarifies that the full tensor is never constructed and processing is sequential. The asymptotic claim is correct, but the phrasing warrants slight clarification to prevent misunderstanding of the memory footprint during implementation.

comments.title: Vague mapping between temperature τ and random feature variance σ²
comments.quote: Eq. 5 assumes both the query and keys are of norm-1. It therefore approximates a softmax attention that normalizes the queries and keys before multiplying them, and then scales the logits by dividing them by _σ_[2] . ... _σ_ is learned, but random vectors _w_i_ are _not_ _d_-dimensional
comments.explanation: The authors state that `σ^2` corresponds to the temperature term `τ` in Eq. 1. In standard Random Fourier Features (Rahimi & Recht, 2007), the distribution of random weights `ω` depends on the kernel's bandwidth parameter (related to `τ`). The paper treats `σ^2` as a direct scaling factor for the logits, which is a practical and common approximation but technically conflates the softmax temperature scaling with the RFF kernel bandwidth parameter. While empirically sound, the theoretical justification is glossed over. A brief note on how `τ` translates to the variance of the Gaussian sampling for `ω` would improve rigor and align with standard RFF theory.

comments.title: Misattributed reference for long-sequence speedup claim
comments.quote: For example, RFA does _not_ achieve any speedup when working with 512-length context ( _§_ 4.1), but achieves a 5 _._ 3 _×_ speedup with 4,000-length context ( _§_ 4.2).
comments.explanation: The authors attribute a 5.3× speedup on 4,000-length context to § 4.2 (Machine Translation). However, the MT datasets evaluated in § 4.2 (WMT14 EN-DE/EN-FR and IWSLT14 DE-EN) consist of relatively short sentences and do not contain 4,000-length contexts. The experiments demonstrating significant speedups on long sequences are actually presented in § 4.3 (Long Text Classification), which is cut off from the provided context. This reference mismatch could mislead readers about where the long-sequence empirical results are reported and may cause confusion during reproduction or comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in space complexity analysis
comments.explanation: Assuming the OCR artifacts represent summation symbols ($\sum_i$), the text claims the space complexity comes from storing '$\sum_i \phi(k_i) \otimes v_i$' and '$\sum_i \phi(k_i)$'. In complexity analysis, this notation is slightly misleading because the summation implies an aggregate over all sequence positions $i$, which could be misread as requiring $O(M)$ space to store intermediate sums for each $i$. The authors actually store only the *accumulated state variables* $S_t$ and $z_t$, which are updated recursively in $O(1)$ space per step. While Footnote 8 clarifies that the full tensor is never explicitly constructed, the main text's phrasing conflates the mathematical expression of the attention sum with the memory footprint of the recurrent state. Stating that it stores the running totals $S_t$ and $z_t$ would be more precise and align better with the $O(1)$ space claim per timestep.

comments.title: Minor numerical phrasing discrepancy in results
comments.quote: From the last row of Table 1 we see that this brings a more than 1.5 test perplexity improvement.
comments.explanation: According to Table 1, the 'Big' RFA-GATE-Gaussian model has a test perplexity of 25.0, while the stateful variant (last row) achieves 23.5. The difference is exactly 1.5, not 'more than 1.5'. This is a minor imprecision in the prose description of the table, likely due to rounding or loose phrasing, but it does not alter the authors' conclusion that the stateful variant provides a meaningful boost.

comments.title: Overloaded symbol: σ used for both temperature parameter and sigmoid activation
comments.explanation: I initially noticed that the symbol σ is used to denote the learnable temperature parameter throughout Sections 3.1 and 3.3 (replacing the fixed τ). However, in this equation, σ is clearly functioning as the sigmoid activation function to produce gates in the range (0, 1). This creates a direct notational clash. In the field, it is standard to distinguish the temperature/softmax scale parameter from the sigmoid activation function (e.g., using σ(·) for the function and τ or λ for the scale). A careful reader might misinterpret whether the temperature parameter is being passed through the gating function, or simply confuse the two distinct concepts. While the mathematical intent is recoverable from context, the overloading is poor notation practice that could lead to implementation bugs or confusion when reading closely.

comments.title: Underspecified recurrence dynamics for the gating mechanism
comments.quote: By multiplying the learned scalar gates 0 < g_t < 1 against the hidden state ( **S** _t,_ **z** _t_ ), history is exponentially decayed, favoring more recent context.
comments.explanation: The authors describe the effect of the gating mechanism on the hidden state but do not provide the explicit update equations. Phrasing it as 'multiplying ... against the hidden state (S_t, z_t)' is slightly ambiguous—it is unclear whether the gate multiplies the state at time t before or after the new information φ(q_t) ⊗ v_t is added. Standard gated recurrences update the previous state first (S_t = g_t * S_{t-1} + φ(q_t) ⊗ v_t). Without the precise recurrence formula in the main text, a reader attempting to reproduce the gated RFA variant would have to guess the order of operations (decay-then-add vs. add-then-decay), which can affect gradient flow and numerical stability. I checked Section 3.2 and the provided context, and while the description is conceptually clear, the algorithmic step is left implicit.

comments.title: Missing gating mechanism formula and state update rule
comments.quote:
augment RFA with a learned gating mechanism (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014; Peng et al., 2018, inter alia):

**w** _g_ and _bg_ are learned parameters, and **x** _t_ is the input representation at timestep _t_ .[4] By multiplying the learned scalar gates 0 _< gt <_ 1 against the hidden state ( **S** _t,_ **z** _t_ ), history is exponentially decayed, favoring more recent context.
comments.explanation: I initially expected to see a formal equation defining the gating mechanism `g_t` and the recurrent update for `(S_t, z_t)` right after the colon, as this is the defining feature of the RFA-GATE variant. However, the mathematical formulation is entirely absent; the text jumps straight to describing the parameters and qualitatively explaining the decay. While the prose conveys the intuition (multiplying a scalar gate against the hidden state), it does not specify the exact update rule (e.g., `S_t = g_t S_{t-1} + ...` vs. `S_t = exp(-g_t) S_{t-1} + ...`), nor does it define the gate's internal structure. Without this, a reader cannot accurately implement or reproduce the GATE variant, leaving a core contribution underspecified.

comments.title: Garbled OCR artifact obscures random weight distribution definition
comments.quote: **I** vector _d_ is the _**σ**_ _d_ is learned, but random vectors _× d_ identity matrix, and _◦_ denotes elementwise product between vectors. **w**  _i_ are _not_ .[5] _d_ -dimensional
comments.explanation: Upon reading the paragraph following the temperature reparameterization discussion, I was confused by the nonsensical syntax and stray symbols. It quickly became apparent that this is a severe OCR corruption of the mathematical definition for the random weight initialization `w_i`. Based on Footnote 5 and the surrounding prose, the authors intend to define a learned, non-isotropic variance vector `σ_d` such that `w_i ~ N(0, σ_d ⊙ I_d)`. While the intent is recoverable from context, the broken equation disrupts the formal derivation and leaves the exact reparameterization scheme and initialization distribution ambiguous. A clean, explicit formula is necessary for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for temperature parameter (σ vs σ²)
comments.quote: It therefore approximates a softmax attention that normalizes the queries and keys before multiplying them, and then scales the logits by dividing them by _σ_[2] . Empirically, this normalization step scales down the logits (Vaswani et al., 2017) and enforces that _−_ 1 _≤_ **q** _[⊤]_ **k** _≤_ 1. In consequence, the softmax outputs would be “flattened” if not for _σ_ , which can be set _a priori_ as a hyperparameter...
comments.explanation: The text states that logits are scaled by 'dividing by σ²', yet the notation summary and surrounding context identify 'σ' as the temperature hyperparameter τ (replacing it via reparameterization). In standard attention formulations, temperature divides the logits directly (`logits / τ`). Using `σ²` implies a squared scaling relationship (`τ = σ²` or `τ = 1/σ²`), which is non-standard and mathematically distinct. This discrepancy could easily mislead practitioners replicating the training procedure, as the effective temperature and softmax sharpness would differ from the canonical formulation. It appears to be a notation inconsistency or a lingering artifact from variance parameterization (`σ²` is conventionally variance), but it remains unclarified in the text.

comments.title: Numerical contradiction between text claim and Table 1
comments.quote: Notably, RFA-GATE-Gaussian outperforms BASE under both size settings by at least 1.2 perplexity.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the gated Gaussian variant outperforms the BASE model by at least 1.2 perplexity for both small and big models. However, Table 1 reports a test perplexity of 26.3 for RFA-GATE-Gaussian (Big) versus 26.2 for BASE (Big), meaning it actually underperforms by 0.1 on the big test set. The claim correctly holds for the small models (32.7 vs 34.5), but the explicit phrasing 'under both size settings' creates a direct factual contradiction with the provided data. comments.title: Missing or severely garbled equations disrupting mathematical derivations
comments.quote: Let φ: R^d → R^{2D} be a nonlinear transformation: Variance of the estimation is inversely proportional to D (Appendix A.2; Yu et al., 2016).

In Section 2.2, Theorem 1 introduces a nonlinear transformation but immediately jumps to discussing estimation variance without displaying the random feature map formula. Similarly, Sections 3.1 and 3.2 reference Eq. 5 and the gating mechanism but lack the corresponding mathematical expressions. While the provided summary successfully reconstructs these formulas, a reader or reproducer working solely from the main text will find the logical flow between kernel approximation, unbiased estimation, and the final RFA recurrence broken. The missing notation hinders verification of the O(M) complexity claims and the causal recurrence derivation.

comments.quote: Here we instead learn it from data with the reparameterization trick (Kingma & Welling, 2014): I vector d is the σ d is learned, but random vectors × d identity matrix, and ◦ denotes elementwise product between vectors. w i are not .[5] d-dimensional
comments.explanation: The paragraph in Section 3.3 explaining the learned temperature parameter σ is heavily corrupted by OCR noise, including fragmented LaTeX, stray symbols, and disjointed syntax. Footnote 5 attempts to clarify that this departs from the isotropic assumption by making σ_d learned, but the main text is nearly unreadable. Although the paper summary correctly identifies the intended reparameterization σ = exp(σ̃), the garbled prose obscures how the elementwise temperature scales the logits versus the random projections. This limits clarity on the variance reduction mechanism and implementation details without requiring the reader to constantly cross-reference the summary or appendix.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated approach to linearizing transformer attention via random feature methods, and the writing is generally clear and logically structured. The key strength is the principled derivation of RFA from an unbiased kernel estimate, which provides a strong theoretical foundation, and the optional gating mechanism is a thoughtful addition to address recency bias. However, the clarity could be improved in a few places: the connection between the random feature map in Theorem 1 and the subsequent approximation of exp(⟨·,·⟩) is stated but could benefit from a more explicit step-by-step walkthrough for readers less familiar with kernel methods. A more significant issue is the potential ambiguity around the "drop-in replacement" claim—while the linear complexity is theoretically attractive, the practical implementation details (e.g., the choice of D, numerical stability, and handling of the denominator in the softmax approximation) are not fully addressed in the exposition. Furthermore, the paper would benefit from a clearer early articulation of how RFA differs from and improves upon prior linear attention works (e.g., Katharopoulos et al.) beyond just the random feature perspective; the current comparison is somewhat deferred. Overall, the paper is of high quality and the core idea is novel, but the presentation could be made more self-contained and the practical advantages more sharply delineated from existing efficient attention alternatives.

comments.title: Speedup claim inconsistency between abstract and Section 3.4
comments.quote:
In the machine translation experiment, RFA decodes twice as fast as a vanilla transformer. (Abstract)
For example, 1.9 × speedup is achieved in our machine translation experiments ( § 4.2); (Section 3.4)
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'twice as fast' (2×), while the complexity analysis later states a 1.9× speedup. Although the difference is small, a technical paper should be precise in its central claims. The abstract may be rounding, but this could mislead a reader comparing performance. The context does not resolve the discrepancy because no further explanation is given for the rounding.

comments.title: Garbled description of learned random feature variance (the σ vector and identity matrix)
comments.quote: **I** vector _d_ is the _**σ** d_ is learned, but random vectors _× d_ identity matrix, and _◦_ denotes elementwise product between vectors. **w**  _i_ are _not_ .[5] _d_ -dimensional
comments.explanation: This sentence is heavily garbled by OCR and does not convey the intended equation for learning the random feature variance. From the context and footnote [5], it is clear the authors intended to write something like 'I_d is the d×d identity matrix, σ is a learned vector, w_i are random vectors, and ◦ denotes elementwise product.' However, as presented, a reader cannot reconstruct the exact formula. Since the reparameterization trick for learning σ is a key contribution (distinguishing from isotropic assumptions), this lack of clarity hinders reproducibility. The surrounding prose partially clarifies, but the missing equation is a moderate gap.

comments.title: Missing definition of the random feature map in Theorem 1 and incomplete arc-cosine kernel definition
comments.quote:
**Theorem 1** (Rahimi & Recht, 2007) **.** _Let_ _**φ**_ : R _[d] →_ R[2] _[D] be a nonlinear transformation:
...
_**φ**_ arccos( **x** ) = ~~~~ 1 _/D_ [ReLU( **w** 1 _·_ **x** ) _, . . . ,_ ReLU( **w** _D ·_ **x** )] _[⊤]_
comments.explanation: The statement of Theorem 1 is truncated—the actual nonlinear transformation is not given. Similarly, the arc-cosine feature map contains garbled characters ('~~~~ 1 _/D_') that likely represent a scaling factor like '√(1/D)'. Without these definitions, the derivation of the unbiased estimate and the exact form of the feature maps used in experiments are unclear. While the original PDF presumably contains the full formulas, the OCR text fails to provide them, which could confuse readers relying on this excerpt. This qualifies as a moderate technical omission.

comments.title: Imprecise claim about unbiased estimate from Theorem 1
comments.quote: RFA builds on an unbiased estimate to exp(⟨·,·⟩) from Theorem 1
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 (Rahimi & Recht, 2007) as stated in the paper summary provides an unbiased estimate for the Gaussian kernel exp(-||x-y||²/2), not for exp(⟨x,y⟩) directly. Although assuming unit query and key vectors makes exp(⟨x,y⟩) proportional to the Gaussian kernel (with a constant factor e), the passage does not mention this constant or the adjustment. A reader might think the feature map inner product directly approximates exp(⟨x,y⟩) without scaling, which is a minor inaccuracy. The context later assumes unit vectors, but the claim itself is slightly overstated.

comments.title: Incorrect section reference for 5.3× speedup claim
comments.quote: but achieves a 5.\_3\_× speedup with 4,000-length context ( \_§\_ 4.2)
comments.explanation: The passage claims a 5.3× speedup with 4,000-length context and cites §4.2 (Machine Translation). However, the machine translation experiments in §4.2 use WMT14 and IWSLT14 datasets, which have short source and target sequences (typically hundreds of tokens). No speedup of 5.3× is reported in that section; the paper's abstract and §5 mention 12× for 2,048-length outputs, and the long text classification experiments (likely §4.3) contain the 5.3× result. The reference to §4.2 is inconsistent with the actual content of that section and would mislead a reader about which experiment supports the speedup claim.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript introduces "Random Feature Attention" (RFA), a computationally efficient variant of the Transformer attention mechanism that approximates the softmax function using kernel random features. The paper is well-motivated and clearly addresses the growing demand for reduced memory footprint and quadratic runtime in sequence modeling. The writing is generally clear, presenting a solid theoretical foundation based on Rahimi and Recht’s random Fourier features and successfully connecting this to the Transformer architecture. However, the introduction is somewhat verbose, and the primary significant issue to investigate further is the *exact balance of approximation error versus computational gain*. While the authors claim significant speedups, the specific hyperparameters governing the random feature maps (e.g., the number of features $D$) and how these choices affect model accuracy on varying sequence lengths require careful scrutiny, particularly to substantiate the claim that RFA outperforms other efficient baselines for "moderate-length" sequences where overheads often negate savings.

comments.title: Missing hyphen in description of sequence length

comments.title: Ambiguity in memory complexity phrasing
comments.explanation: The sentence opens by stating that RFA uses O(4D + 2Dd) space to store φ(q_t) and a list of other components (indicated by the garbled OCR text, likely the hidden state S_t and z_t). This phrasing implies that the total memory complexity of RFA is O(4D + 2Dd), which would be incorrect as it must also scale with the sequence length M (to store the feature map of keys). The later sentence clarifies the comparison to softmax (O(Md)) and suggests that the 'lower memory overhead' exists when 2D ≪ M, but the initial sentence conflates the recurrent state cost with the total cost, making it ambiguous for a reader to distinguish between the 'constant' overhead and the M-dependent cost.

comments.title: Incorrect calculation of average accuracy comparison
comments.quote: Averaging across three datasets, RFA outperforms the transformer by 0.3% accuracy, second only to Zaheer et al. (2020) with a 0.1% accuracy gap.
comments.explanation: The passage makes specific numerical claims about the accuracy averages of the Transformer baseline and the RFA-Gaussian model. However, the numbers presented in Table 3 contradict these claims. Calculating the average accuracy from the table: Transformer (36.4 + 64.3 + 57.5) / 3 = 52.73%, while RFA-Gaussian (36.8 + 66.0 + 53.0) / 3 = 51.93%. The difference is approximately -0.80%, meaning RFA *underperforms* the Transformer on average, not by 0.3%, but by nearly one percentage point. The text also states RFA is second only to Zaheer et al., but Zaheer's average (52.0%) is actually higher than RFA's (51.93%), making the ranking claim valid but the quantitative justification flawed. This represents a significant factual error in the summary of experimental results.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation regarding feature map size D
comments.quote: This is because when using the same D (as we do here), the φ arccos is half the size of φ Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The notation summary defines φ as mapping from ℝ^d to ℝ^D, implying D is the output dimension. However, in Section 3.3, the Gaussian kernel feature map typically doubles the dimension (using sin and cos pairs), whereas the arc-cosine map uses ReLU pairs without doubling. The text asserts that when using the same 'D' (the number of random samples/hyperparameter), the arc-cosine map is 'half the size'. If 'D' refers to the input hyperparameter (number of random weights), the claim is consistent (input D samples for Arc-cosine produces D-dimensional output; input D samples for Gaussian produces 2D-dimensional output). However, if 'D' strictly refers to the output dimension as per the main definition, the statement is ambiguous without clarification that 'same D' refers to the sample count, not the mapped dimension. This could confuse readers relying on the explicit function notation.

comments.title: Imprecise complexity comparison in Related Work
comments.quote: Reminiscent of RNN language models, RFA attends beyond a fixed context window through a stateful computation, without increasing time or memory overhead.
comments.explanation: The authors are contrasting RFA with 'memory-bounded' approaches (like fixed-window sparse attention), which saturate in memory/time as the sequence grows. However, the statement 'without increasing time or memory overhead' is technically inaccurate because RFA has $O(N)$ time and memory complexity; the overhead *does* increase linearly with sequence length. The authors likely mean 'without the overhead [growing quadratically / hitting a hard bottleneck]'. While the intent is clear from the preceding sentence about fixed-memory modules, the phrasing is misleading and could be corrected to reflect that RFA scales linearly rather than having zero overhead growth.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology for 'Reception field'
comments.quote: The idea behind these methods is to limit the reception field of attention computation.
comments.explanation: In the context of deep learning and transformers, the standard term for the size of the part of the input sequence a neuron or layer can see is the 'receptive field'. 'Reception field' is not a standard term and could be interpreted as a typo or confusion with another field (e.g., broadcasting). While context suggests the intended meaning, it is an unnecessary linguistic stumble.

comments.title: Dimensional Inconsistency in Causal RFA State Update Equation
comments.quote: S_t = S_{t-1} ⊗ φ(k_t) + φ(q_t) ⊗ φ(v_t)
comments.explanation: The text defines the hidden state update for causal RFA (specifically in the gated variant context) as S_t = S_{t-1} ⊗ φ(k_t) + φ(q_t) ⊗ φ(v_t). This formulation is technically flawed on two counts: (1) Conceptually, the state S_t represents the accumulation of past Key-Value pairs to approximate dot products with the *current* query. Adding the query (φ(q_t)) to this accumulated history is incorrect; the history should only update with past keys (φ(k_i)). (2) Dimensionally, assuming standard shapes where S_t and φ(q_t) are of size [2D, d] and v_t is [d, d], the term φ(q_t) ⊗ φ(v_t) would result in a tensor of size [2D, d*d]. This cannot be added to S_{t-1} of size [2D, d]. The correct update in standard randomized attention (e.g., Performers) is S_t = S_{t-1} + φ(k_t) ⊗ φ(v_t) (or a similar factorized update). The OCR likely misread 'k_t' as 'q_t' or the symbol for 'v' was garbled, but as presented, the equation prevents the reader from understanding or reproducing the model's recurrence mechanism.

comments.title: Misstatement Regarding ℓ_2-Norm of Gaussian Feature Maps
comments.quote: We conjecture that this is because the outputs of the Gaussian feature maps have an ℓ_2-norm of 1, which can help stabilize training. To see why, sin[2] ( _x_ ) + cos[2] ( _x_ ) = cos( _x − x_ ) = 1.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the ℓ_2-norm of the Gaussian random feature map outputs is 1. However, for a standard random feature approximation using sinusoids (e.g., [cos(w_1·x), sin(w_1·x), ..., cos(w_D·x), sin(w_D·x)]), the squared norm is the sum of the squared terms. Since sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 for each dimension, the squared norm of the feature vector is ∑1 = D, and the ℓ_2-norm is √D. The vector does not have a unit norm unless D = 1. The authors' derivation correctly identifies that individual basis functions have unit norm, but they erroneously conclude that the *output vectors* (the entire feature map) have unit norm. This is a conceptual error in the justification provided.

progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript introduces "Random Feature Attention" (RFA), a computationally efficient variant of the Transformer attention mechanism that approximates the softmax function using kernel random features. The paper is well-motivated and clearly addresses the growing demand for reduced memory footprint and quadratic runtime in sequence modeling. The writing is generally clear, presenting a solid theoretical foundation based on Rahimi and Recht’s random Fourier features and successfully connecting this to the Transformer architecture. However, the introduction is somewhat verbose, and the primary significant issue to investigate further is the *exact balance of approximation error versus computational gain*. While the authors claim significant speedups, the specific hyperparameters governing the random feature maps (e.g., the number of features $D$) and how these choices affect model accuracy on varying sequence lengths require careful scrutiny, particularly to substantiate the claim that RFA outperforms other efficient baselines for "moderate-length" sequences where overheads often negate savings.
